I thought I was in The Matrix: opening CMD for the first time
During my house-arrest period, I'd play Flash games at home (
) and also some online games. The main one was
— yeah, the one with the famous line:
Husband, I'm going to military service!
for the first time in ages. I've played this since elementary school, all the way past entering the workforce — still pop in occasionally for nostalgia (veteran player confirmed).
It's the so-called "trap" meme — male players controlling female characters. Back then,
's character gender wasn't player-chosen at account signup — it was decided by Taiwan ID number. So female characters were relatively rare.
of Taiwan ID = gender code: 1 = male, 2 = female. (First character is a letter representing the issuing region.)
The reason this became a joke: early
was huge on "online husbands / online wives" (in-game couples). Usually the online husband would level you, give you gear, run dungeons with you — basically hand-feed you everything.

So a lot of guys would use a relative's ID number (sister, cousin, etc.) to register a female character and look for an online husband. Many guys ended up scammed by these "fake girls", spawning the classic joke.
Actually Taiwan ID numbers follow patterns, so tools to generate "valid IDs" sprang up online.
Story aside, back to the topic. While playing
, since my dad banned internet cafés, I was stuck at home — and a problem cropped up...
"Weird, why is it so Lag? Previous days were fine, why is today especially Lag?" I thought.
I opened the browser and tried Yahoo! homepage — page load was painfully slow. Then I remembered: a senior at the café taught me a command to check network status. So I tried CMD.
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
was around 2000ms — normal should be under 100ms. Not sure what to do, I restarted the router to see if it helped.
After restart, the network was smooth — but then a yell came from the rest of the house. They'd been BT-downloading files...
The Command Prompt (CMD) is Windows' built-in terminal, letting users operate the computer via text commands — file management, system settings, network diagnostics, etc.
Through this interface, we can run various commands. The
I mentioned checks network connectivity. There are also
, etc. that help analyze network status. Below I'll cover the common CMD commands. Usage is simple: type the command in the CMD window and press Enter.
The GIF above was recorded on Mac — Windows recording was inconvenient, apologies.
Modern Windows leans toward PowerShell (newer terminal), but most commands are similar. On Mac, since it's Unix-based, commands differ.
Test connectivity speed and stability to a site or IP
Find current IP, DNS, gateway info
Check for unknown programs accessing network
The above are basic network-diagnostic commands. When you hit network issues, try these first.
The terminal has many more commands —
, etc., mostly for file system operations. Skipping those here.
Side-share — some fun terminal easter eggs. Different OSes (Windows, Linux) have unexpected effects from specific commands.
— turn the terminal text green, instant Matrix vibes. (Note: PowerShell doesn't support.)
— show the directory structure as a tree. (Run in a folder with subdirectories, otherwise screen gets flooded with
— an ASCII train chugs across your terminal.
People say Linux and Mac environments are close, so commands are similar. Truth is there are differences.
aren't installed by default on Mac.
To try them on Mac, install Homebrew first, then install these commands via Homebrew:
Homebrew is the mainstream third-party package manager on macOS
Mac OS Terminal Beautification — One Article Covers It
(article in Mandarin) for detailed install instructions.
Did you ever try opening a terminal as a kid?
Watching streams of code flow across the screen, I felt like a hacker — too cool XD.

A network-troubleshooting story from my first time opening CMD — basic uses of ping, ipconfig, tracert, netstat with macOS equivalents, plus terminal easter eggs and Homebrew (sl, cowsay, fortune, cmatrix). A Lag incident that strings together the practicality and fun of the command line.
